title: Replace single-step target learning with a discrete hazard curve over the next 1-8 laps
summary: Discrete-time survival work shows binary classifiers can model per-interval hazard effectively, and recent F1 pit-stop work found value in joint next-lap classification plus stop-horizon estimation.
rationale: Discrete-time survival work shows binary classifiers can model per-interval hazard effectively, and recent F1 pit-stop work found value in joint next-lap classification plus stop-horizon estimation. A full hazard curve is a stronger formulation than a single next-lap label because it teaches the model where the pit window is approaching, not just whether it fires immediately.
implementation_hint: Create person-period rows for horizons h=1..8 per original lap, mark the first future pit as the event and later horizons as censored after a stop/finish, train a GBDT/CatBoost with horizon as a feature, then recover next-lap probability from h=1 while optionally using summed survival consistency as an auxiliary check.
expected_effect: Better ranking near pit windows, especially for borderline laps where current features know a stop is close but not whether it is exactly next lap.
risk: Dataset expansion can be memory-heavy, and incorrect censoring/label construction will silently leak future information.
